title: Netflix quietly reveals what AI did to 300 of its programs
excerpt: Every movie you have ever loved has lied to you. The city skyline was a matte painting. The shark was rubber and it barely worked. The crowd of 40,000 was 40...
content_html:
Every movie you have ever loved has lied to you.

The city skyline was a matte painting. The shark was rubber and it barely worked. The crowd of 40,000 was 400 extras and a lot of patient duplication. Audiences have never actually wanted the truth from a screen. We want the trick to hold.

Hollywood has always been comfortable with that bargain. What it has never been comfortable with is a change in who performs the trick.

That is the fight that shut the industry down in 2023, and it is the fight that has kept studios careful about how they discuss artificial intelligence (AI) ever since. Everyone has been running pilots. Almost nobody has been willing to say how many. The safe play was to call it experimentation, name a single project, and move on before anyone asked a follow-up question.

Netflix (NFLX) just stopped playing it safe.

The streamer told shareholders in its second-quarter letter that roughly 300 of its titles used generative AI somewhere in production this year, with most of that work landing in post-production, according to Variety.

That is not a pilot. That is a supply chain.

What Netflix told its shareholders about AI

The letter described the technology showing up across the full production process, from concept and previsualization through post-production and release. Netflix said the tools help it deliver "higher-quality output more quickly and at a lower cost," according to Variety.

Three titles got named on the second-quarter earnings call: the Indian sports thriller "Glory," the Brazilian soccer miniseries "Brasil 70: A Saga do Tri," and the American Revolution docuseries "The American Experiment."

Co-CEO Ted Sarandos said the tools handled "really complicated shots and sequences," including enhanced crowds and historical battle scenes, according to Deadline.

Related: Netflix may borrow older TV playbook to solve its newest problem

The most specific claim involved that docuseries. It contains 17 minutes of AI-assisted footage produced "twice as fast and at half the cost," per Fortune.

Netflix does have guardrails on paper. Production partners must flag intended AI use, and generating performances or replacing union-covered work requires consent and the right agreements, according to Digital Trends.

What struck me working through the earnings coverage was the order of the argument. Sarandos led with creative scope and reached cost second. AI "will give creatives better tools to bring their visions to life," he said, according to IndieWire.

That sequencing is a choice, and it is the tell. Cost is the part shareholders are paying for.

content_html_zh:
你曾经喜欢过的每一部电影都对你撒了谎。

城市的天际线就像一幅哑光画。 鲨鱼是橡胶的，几乎不起作用。 40,000 名观众中有 400 名临时演员和大量重复的病人。 观众从来没有真正想要从屏幕上看到真相。 我们希望这个技巧能够持续下去。

好莱坞一直对这种讨价还价感到满意。 它从未感到舒服的是表演者的变化。

这场斗争导致该行业在 2023 年停摆，也正是这场斗争让工作室从那时起就在讨论人工智能 (AI) 时保持谨慎。 每个人都在进行试点。 几乎没有人愿意说出有多少。 安全的做法是称之为实验，命名一个项目，然后在有人提出后续问题之前继续前进。

Netflix（NFLX）刚刚不再谨慎行事。

据《Variety》报道，该流媒体在第二季度的信函中告诉股东，今年约有 300 部作品在制作过程中使用了生成式人工智能，其中大部分工作都在后期制作中完成。

那不是飞行员。 那就是供应链。

Netflix 向股东讲述的有关人工智能的内容

这封信描述了整​​个制作过程中出现的技术，从概念和视觉预览到后期制作和发布。 据《Variety》报道，Netflix 表示，这些工具有助于其“更快、以更低的成本提供更高质量的输出”。

第二季度财报电话会议上提名了三部影片：印度体育惊悚片《荣耀》、巴西足球迷你剧《巴西 70：铁人三项赛》和美国革命纪录片《美国实验》。

据 Deadline 报道，联合首席执行官特德·萨兰多斯 (Ted Sarandos) 表示，这些工具可以处理“非常复杂的镜头和序列”，包括增强的人群和历史战斗场景。

相关：Netflix可能会借用旧的电视剧剧本来解决其最新问题

最具体的主张涉及该系列纪录片。 据《财富》报道，它包含 17 分钟的人工智能辅助镜头，“速度提高了一倍，成本减半”。

Netflix确实在纸面上有护栏。 根据 Digital Trends 的说法，制作合作伙伴必须标记预期的人工智能使用，并且产生表演或替换工会涵盖的工作需要征得同意和正确的协议。

在阅读收益报告时，让我印象深刻的是争论的顺序。 萨兰多斯以创意范围领先，成本位居第二。 据 IndieWire 报道，他表示，人工智能“将为创意人员提供更好的工具，将他们的愿景变为现实”。

排序是一种选择，也是一种说明。 成本是股东支付的部分。
